title: TBM 402: The Real-World Journey to Value and Product-Centricity
questions: What does a product operating model maturity journey really look like in practice?
How do organizations move from delivery predictability to outcome-centricity without conflating work, goals, and value?
Why do OKRs fail when they are layered onto work rather than grounded in a shared value model?
How should tech and product teams align org design, funding, architecture, and value creation?
search_terms: work first vs goals first okrs
four graphs intent context collaboration investment
summary: The post lays out a real-world, non-linear narrative for how organizations evolve toward product-centricity, moving from work-centric delivery predictability to goals-first intent and finally toward converging value models that anchor org design, investment, and architecture. It is aimed at product, technology, and transformation leaders who are trying to understand what “good” looks like beyond simplistic maturity models and why OKRs or product operating model frameworks often misfire without the right context.

title: TBM 390: Governance by Principle, Not by Template
questions: Do accounting standards require project and program codes and dollar-precise internal reporting?
How can a company govern technology investments without forcing everything into project/program templates?
What’s the difference between procedural legitimacy and substantive legitimacy in governance?
How should governance change for products and platforms versus static, predictable work?
search_terms: project and program coding GAAP IFRS
summary: This post argues that technology governance should be built from first principles and principles of risk management rather than enforced through legacy project/program templates. It’s aimed at product and platform leaders and builders who feel their organizations are performing procedural box-ticking with misleading precision that harms decision quality. The post outlines how to govern durable investment units (capabilities/products/platforms) using lifecycle-aware oversight, balanced leading/lagging metrics, and portfolio risk thinking.

title: TBM 382: Product-Centricity When You Don't Sell A Digital Product
questions: How can a non-digital-product company become more product-centric when it can’t easily link tech work to revenue or cost?
What determines how long an organization can sustain commitment to product-like investments (confidence horizon)?
Why do “projects to products” and “outputs to outcomes” slogans often fail in large enterprises?
What structural changes are needed when flow optimization reaches its limit due to dependencies?
search_terms: translation layer product vs project funding
you fund teams but how funding flows
summary: The post explores why non-digital-product-selling enterprises find it difficult to adopt product-centric ways of working, focusing on the challenge of linking investments to economic drivers and maintaining fast feedback. It argues that common transformations fail when project/program governance persists, creating a “translation layer” and hitting limits with flow optimization under deep dependencies. It proposes a networked, ecosystem-based operating approach that uses multiple organizational lenses and designs the connective tissue for coherent funding and action across intent, collaboration, and outcomes.

title: Two POM Transformation Flavors
questions: Which product operating model tactics fit a rapid scale-up versus a large enterprise?
Why do feature factories stop working as companies scale?
How do you shift investment and funding from project-centric accounting to product-centric progress?
What prevents enterprise product transformations from taking hold despite adopting modern product language?
summary: The post contrasts two transformation contexts—rapid scale-ups and large enterprises—arguing that each requires different high-leverage moves to adopt a product operating model. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders trying to scale product practices beyond early Agile or “product 1.0” efforts, especially when organizational and funding structures resist change. The core guidance is to align investment and operating structure for scale-ups, while enterprises should prioritize architecture/refactoring to unlock autonomy and eliminate the finance/program translation layer.

title: TBM 358: The Genius of SVPG
questions: How do you pitch organizational change so executives can absorb it without losing face?
Why do internal change efforts get discounted even when the content is correct?
What structural constraints prevent product operating model adoption?
How do you run transformation while keeping the organization operating day to day?
search_terms: no one gets fired for recommending Transformed
continuous improvement in high-performing tech teams
summary: The post argues that SVPG and Transformed are unusually effective at driving product and organizational change because they use executive-friendly narrative framing and timing. It’s aimed at internal change agents and product leaders who need practical guidance on how to get buy-in, run transformation in parallel with day-to-day work, and address structural constraints—not just skills. It also broadens the change lens by contrasting stewardship-style transformation with slower, messier approaches like sensemaking and learning.

title: Chance To Work Together
questions: How can a platform-first product validate itself through direct involvement with early customers?
What does it look like to act as an extended product operations team for a select group of companies?
How do you shift from project funding to product/team funding in practice?
How should organizations model teams and planning cycles (QBR/WBR) to empower frontline execution?
search_terms: QBR WBR planning and review cycles
empower frontline teams with leadership context
summary: The post describes a product strategy for pressure testing a platform by working 1:1 with early customers as an extended product operations team. It’s aimed at product leaders and change agents who want to shift funding models, improve planning/review cadences, and model organizational structures to empower frontline teams with coherent leadership context.

title: A Chance To Work Together
questions: How can we treat our operating model and planning process as a product?
What tools and frameworks help with org design, strategy deployment, and enterprise planning at scale?
How do we move from project/program management to team/outcome management?
What’s the best way to pressure-test an org-operations platform with real teams?
search_terms: treat how we work as a product
summary: The post invites product operations and strategy leaders to partner with Dotwork to pressure-test a platform that helps companies treat how they work as a product. It targets larger technology orgs that already have front-line roadmapping tools but rely on docs/spreadsheets or no-code tools for org design, goals, enterprise planning, and operating rhythms like WBRs/QBRs. The pitch frames the partnership as a high-touch, non-custom “crack team” approach to validate product-forward ways of working.

title: TBM 355: Who Should Care?
questions: Whose job is it to care about operational excellence and ways of working?
Who should reign in the operational aspects of execution that impact outcomes?
Is it better to have “pirates” (fast, pragmatic) or “slow-moving tankers” (process-driven) in product organizations?
What catalyst causes leaders to truly care about operational excellence in a company?
search_terms: PMs going rogue ways of working
pirate ships vs tankers org design
bias to action vs systems thinking
who owns ways of working in product
summary: The post argues that some product leaders and PMs are naturally myopic about company operations, focusing on impact and removing blockers, and that this can be beneficial. It then challenges organizations to ensure someone with the right skills owns operational excellence and continuous improvement to prevent decentralized “pirate ships” and restore alignment during tough times. It’s aimed at product leaders and PMs deciding who should care about ways of working and how to balance pragmatism with organizational design.

title: TBM 350: Connecting Dots
questions: How do we connect work, outputs, and outcomes without oversimplifying product development?
Why do roadmaps and visibility efforts backfire when teams try to standardize everything?
How should we design an operating system that supports both experimentation and execution?
What should we centralize versus leave to local teams to handle context and variation?
summary: The post offers a graph-based way to reason about messy product development by contrasting project-based planning with bet-driven experimentation and causal chains to outcomes. It’s aimed at product and operations leaders designing operating systems, roadmaps, and portfolio rituals who need coordination without forcing a single uniform process. The core guidance is to centralize only the minimum interfaces for alignment, allow fractal local variation, and treat time horizons and models as concurrent, lossy representations rather than a strict cascade.

title: TBM 340: Path to $
questions: How should we cluster our teams to make ROI conversations more realistic?
What changes are needed to help “far” teams (multiple hops) connect work to customer and business value?
When does a project-factory model (spreadsheet per engineer/project) need to shift toward outcome-based team funding?
How should we interpret dependencies—are they a sign we’re early in forming journey or platform teams?
search_terms: how to shift from PMO to product teams
summary: The post proposes a practical way to think about ROI and funding by classifying teams into archetypes based on proximity to customer/business outcomes, degree of independence vs dependency, and stability vs temporariness. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders trying to move from project-based or aspirational product definitions toward outcome-based team structures that can support credible business cases. It also offers diagnostic prompts for identifying where teams are stuck in “everything and nothing” or “spreadsheet project” modes and what to do to shift them.

title: TBM 338: 4 Core Product OS Design Competencies
questions: What skills and experience do companies need to design and evolve a product operating system?
How do leadership and influence affect whether a product OS gets adopted?
What are common traps when product operations lacks domain experience or systems thinking?
What does “general operations and tooling” look like in practice for product teams?
search_terms: rituals norms artifacts product operating model
workflow mapping and data modeling for product ops
how to design a purpose-built product OS
summary: This post lays out a competency model for companies trying to be intentional about designing a product operating system, aimed at product and operations leaders who want their approach to stick. It argues that success depends on balancing leadership influence, domain experience, systems thinking/design, and general operations/tooling, and it highlights failure modes when any one area is missing.

title: TBM 336: Product OS Design Tips and Principles
questions: How do we design our company’s product operating system so it creates alignment without bureaucracy?
What rituals, artifacts, and interfaces should we standardize (and which should we leave flexible)?
How do we prevent premature convergence, false precision, and over-planning from harming execution?
How do we manage dependencies, cycle lengths, and constraints without creating zombie processes or shadow work?
search_terms: official vs real vs ideal practices
summary: The post offers practical design tips for building a company-level product operating system—how product leaders, managers, and teams interact to align, learn, and execute. It’s aimed at product leaders and cross-functional organizations that want to replace rigid process with intentional rituals, consistent interfaces, and constraints that enable learning while managing dependencies and decision cadence.

title: TBM 328: Beyond Firmographic-Based ICPs
questions: How should a new product leader form an ICP when firmographics aren’t enough?
What dimensions should replace company size/age for ways-of-working products?
How do organizational operating models affect product fit and go-to-market motion?
How can you cluster customers so that success and marketing strategies differ appropriately?
search_terms: customer segmentation dimensions awareness centralization inertia effectiveness
summary: The post explains how to build and refine an ideal customer profile for ways-of-working products by moving beyond firmographics toward organizational psychographics. It’s aimed at product managers and leaders who are new to a company or category and need a repeatable way to converge on actionable segmentation that informs product, GTM, onboarding, and customer success. The author proposes a set of dimensions (awareness, centralization/decentralization, rate of change, perceived effectiveness) and shows how they can be used to cluster customers with different needs.

title: TBM 310: Trees, Journeys, and Loops (and the Functional Model Trap)
questions: How do we create a shared language across product, design, and engineering without siloing into one model?
When should we use capability trees versus customer journeys versus flywheels?
How can we represent both enduring capabilities and the customer experience that evolves over time?
What does it mean to define a growth loop hypothesis for sustainable differentiation?
search_terms: capability tree vs customer journey vs flywheel
event storming for workflow and domain boundaries
Wardley mapping movement on capability trees
summary: The post argues that cross-functional teams should use multiple complementary models—capability trees, customer journeys, and growth flywheels—to build an enduring shared language. It explains what each model is good at, highlights how teams fail when they treat one model as the whole truth, and offers guidance for linking models so product, design, engineering, and business growth hypotheses stay aligned.

title: TBM 300: How to Think About Operations and Enablement
questions: What is the practical difference between operations and enablement in product and design contexts?
How do you decide what to standardize versus what to keep local and experimental?
How do you design enablement so it supports without over-enabling?
How can operations and enablement be managed as a unified capability system with maturation over time?
search_terms: service design thinking for internal teams
summary: This post helps operations and enablement leaders distinguish and integrate operations (operationalization) and enablement (equipping and support) in product, design, and broader organizational contexts. It argues for a holistic, platform-like view of these functions across functional, cross-functional, and general domains, unified through a capability-based framework. It is aimed at practitioners who want practical principles, experimentation guidance, and a strategy-to-tactics approach for building resilient organizational capabilities.

title: TBM 291: Why Your Product Transformation Will Fail
questions: Why do product transformation programs fail even when companies adopt popular frameworks?
How can leaders create conditions for continuous improvement instead of running change as a project?
What should we stop doing (subtractive change) to free time for experimentation and customer impact?
How do architecture and cross-team dependencies block product-focused ways of working?
search_terms: frameworks as training wheels vs systems thinking
summary: This post argues that product transformations fail when leaders run them like conventional projects and undermine team motivation, credibility, and autonomy. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders who want to shift from episodic change to continuous improvement by aligning purpose (“true north”), removing constraints, enabling experimentation, and addressing architectural dependencies. It also warns against framework copy/paste and against transformation messaging that devalues the present or lacks real working examples.

title: TBM 282: Three Stages of Org Evolution
questions: How do organizational characteristics differ across startup, established tech, and modernization phases?
Can a single company move through multiple organizational evolution motions, and what tensions arise during shifts?
What does it mean to modernize an organization that doesn’t primarily sell digital products?
search_terms: startup vs established company operating model
operating model framework for company stages
summary: The post presents a stage-based framework for thinking about how companies evolve, contrasting rapidly growing startups/scale-ups, established tech companies, and non-digital-product businesses modernizing toward digital. It argues that while any model is imperfect, the patterns can help leaders anticipate differences and manage the tensions that occur when organizations shift between motions. It’s aimed at readers designing or evolving operating models and navigating organizational change.

title: TBM 281: Stop Chasing Unicorns
questions: How do we find what the best competitors do differently in day-to-day ways of working?
Which specific capabilities should we strengthen to improve product outcomes?
Where can we run small, incremental experiments instead of chasing unicorn companies?
How do we avoid vague “Agile/product-led” language and define concrete practices?
summary: This post argues that product leaders should stop benchmarking only the rare “unicorn” companies and instead study how their nearest competitors work in practice. It’s for teams trying to improve product performance by defining concrete ways of working—discovery, experimentation, shipping cadence, and decision-making—rather than relying on vague labels or assumptions about hiring. It also addresses how internal change agents can misalign personal goals with the organization’s immediate capability needs.

title: Notes on Product management theater | Marty Cagan (Lenny's Podcast)
questions: How can product leaders identify what’s truly “best” without relying on outside perceptions or survivorship bias?
Why do operating-model copies (e.g., Spotify/Amazon practices) break when moved to new contexts?
Are role-heavy orgs fixing real product-system problems or just patching symptoms?
How should empowerment be implemented so teams have autonomy without devolving into anarchy?
search_terms: reverse anna karenina principle product org
best vs rest product operating model
role inflation product ops agile coach product owner
pandemic overhiring lowered the bar product skills
product transformation less process more return
summary: This post captures a discussion about how product leaders should spread proven product principles while recognizing that context determines how those principles work in practice. It challenges simplistic narratives about “best companies,” role inflation, and pandemic-era hiring, and argues for transformation through leadership-defined bets and structurally supported team empowerment. It’s aimed at product managers and product leaders trying to improve operating models, career growth, and organizational effectiveness without falling into theater or copycat thinking.

title: TBM 255: The New Airbnb (Lenny's Podcast) w/Melissa Perri
questions: How did founder and CPO leadership at Airbnb evolve, and what did they provide to motivate teams at scale?
When did Airbnb realize it was heading toward an “arbitrage business” path, and how was that strategy defined and owned?
Do wartime practices (more control, sharper decisions) remain effective in peacetime, and what should change?
How did Airbnb’s GM model and PM hiring/reward criteria affect cross-domain dependencies and product strategy?
search_terms: GM business unit model pros and cons
power over power with power to power within
summary: This post is a set of leadership and product-management questions prompted by a podcast conversation with Airbnb’s leadership, aimed at readers trying to understand how and why certain practices worked (or didn’t) as the company scaled. It focuses on transferring operating models across contexts, contrasting founder strengths in early-stage building with the mentorship, structure, and empowerment needed for growth. It’s for product leaders and executives who want a framework for evaluating strategy, org design, and PM team empowerment beyond surface-level takeaways.

title: TBM 200: Product Team / Enabling Team Collaboration
questions: How can product teams collaborate more effectively with enabling teams like education, documentation, support, and legal?
What organizational incentives and operating practices cause product to ship and forget while enabling teams become cost centers?
How do we reduce context switching and discontinuity between product and enabling work?
What does “the whole company is the product” mean in day-to-day planning and work tracking?
search_terms: enablement teams incentives and cost center problem
end-to-end customer experience as the product
streams of effort vs projects product management
summary: This post explores why product teams struggle to collaborate with enabling teams (education, documentation, support, marketing, legal, and internal tools) and how those frictions show up in planning, incentives, and day-to-day workflows. It’s aimed at product leaders and cross-functional managers who want practical organizational nudges—like end-to-end work visualization, lower WIP, and explicit collaboration models—to improve customer outcomes in SaaS and similar product organizations.

title: TBM 8/52: The Problem With “Internal Customers”
questions: Should internal product teams call their users “internal customers” or “partners”?
How do b2b2b/b2b2c dynamics change what “customer-centric” means for internal products?
What’s the right collaboration model for shared/platform teams when use cases are still emerging?
How can internal teams avoid optimizing for requested use cases or operational metrics that don’t create external value?
summary: This post argues that “internal customer” is the wrong framing for internal product teams and shared/platform functions, especially in b2b2b/b2b2c SaaS where value depends on helping external customers make money. It recommends treating internal counterparts as partners and embedding with them to ensure eventual external customer value, rather than optimizing for tickets, requested use cases, or internal operational metrics.

title: Product Enablement Principles
questions: How should product enablement leaders balance principles, process, and day-to-day operations?
What practices build trust and credibility across teams during change efforts?
How do you run safe-to-fail experiments while accounting for second- and third-order effects?
When should you operationalize what works versus keep exploring with new experiments?
search_terms: trust formula credibility reliability empathy apparent self-interest
summary: The post lays out practical principles for product enablement and operations, aimed at leaders who help teams improve customer outcomes without burning out morale. It emphasizes principles over rigid process, co-designing change, building trust, and using safe-to-fail experiments within a complex adaptive systems view. It also frames product enablement as company-wide in SaaS, where documentation, support, education, and go-to-market collectively shape customer experience.

title: TBM 46/52: Verticals, Horizontals, and Dimensions
questions: Which customer, workflow, and market dimensions actually matter for our product and go-to-market?
When expanding beyond our initial vertical, how do we estimate how much relevant dimensionality will increase?
How should we handle customization across industries without exploding complexity?
Why do platform strategies sometimes fail to reduce complexity in practice?
summary: The post reframes B2B SaaS positioning from a simple vertical-vs-horizontal dichotomy to a “relevant dimensionality” lens for product and go-to-market strategy. It argues that growth depends on correctly identifying which dimensions matter, incorporating cross-functional perspectives, and anticipating non-linear scaling effects. It’s aimed at product, strategy, and GTM leaders planning expansion, segmentation, and platform approaches.

title: TBM 45/52: What is ProductOps?
questions: What is ProductOps and how is it different from PMO?
Who should be the customer of ProductOps inside an organization?
What kinds of problems are best solved by ProductOps as a cross-team function?
How can ProductOps enable standardization and coordination without harming team autonomy or slowing teams down?
search_terms: second and third order effects in change management
summary: The post defines ProductOps as an internal platform product focused on solving cross-team problems that individual teams cannot address, using collective leverage rather than process enforcement. It argues that ProductOps must be customer-centric (its customers are cross-functional product teams) and should apply product thinking—strategy, systems thinking, service design, and learning-driven experimentation—to avoid harmful second/third-order effects during change. It is aimed at product leaders and operators designing or evaluating a ProductOps function, especially in scaling organizations.

title: TBM 45/52: Taming Model Malpractice
questions: How do we choose the right level of model accuracy for different decision types?
What’s the right balance between having enough models to do the job and not so many that teams can’t align?
How can we prevent global models from becoming unused or overridden by local shadow models?
What governance approach helps maintain consistency across multiple customer and product models?
search_terms: all models are wrong but some are useful
customer health model vs product health model
summary: The post argues that analytics and strategy models should be judged by their usefulness for a specific “job,” not by how accurate they are in absolute terms. It’s aimed at product, marketing, and analytics leaders who struggle with misaligned model usage, translation overhead, and unused global frameworks. It proposes two remedies—hierarchies of complementary models and a platform approach—to balance global consistency with local applicability.

title: TBM 41/52: Product GTM Fitness
questions: How do we balance product and gtm teams as we scale without accumulating debt?
What causes the pendulum swing of complexity burden between product and go-to-market?
How can we grow revenue while managing the cost of operational complexity?
What early warning signals indicate product or gtm fitness is flagging?
summary: This post offers a framework for leaders in b2b saas to understand the recurring tension between product and go-to-market teams during scaling. It argues that revenue growth and operational complexity trade off over time, creating “product debt” or “gtm debt” when the burden shifts or is ignored. It also provides practical tactics (offload, tame, absorb/deal, avoid) and early signals to detect when product-gtm “fitness” is deteriorating.

title: TBM 26/52: Scaled Feature Factories
questions: Why do digital teams in non-digital companies revert to feature factory behavior even when OKRs are used?
How do commitment networks, sunk cost bias, and premature convergence prevent changing course?
What organizational conditions make it hard for product teams to be empowered and outcome-focused?
How can product leaders stop taking it personally and shift from project delivery to product thinking?
search_terms: empowered product teams in non-digital companies
summary: The post explains why digital initiatives in non-digital product companies often devolve into overwhelming, prescriptive feature factories despite attempts to use modern product practices like OKRs. It’s aimed at product leaders and digital transformation stakeholders who need to understand the organizational and incentive dynamics that block empowerment and outcome-focused delivery at scale.

title: TBM 23/52: Your Company = The Product
questions: Where does most of our operational complexity originate?
Which teams absorb the complexity created by product updates?
Are teams aware of how much complexity gets passed on from product work?
How can we build bridges of understanding across functions so product changes land smoothly?
search_terms: product team can't operate in a vacuum
how marketing support legal absorb product changes
summary: The post argues that in b2b saas, you should treat the entire company as “the product” because product improvements create non-linear, multiplied complexity across many functions and customer segments. It’s aimed at product and operations leaders who want to anticipate second- and third-order effects and improve cross-functional alignment when shipping updates.

title: TBM 47/52: Operations (and ProductOps)
questions: How should we define and practice ProductOps given our organizational biases and team constraints?
What operational mindset should we use when scaling a working product versus fixing a broken one?
How do experience levels in operations and product change the way teams approach operational concerns?
When is it better to do nothing and observe versus running process or technology changes?
search_terms: complex adaptive systems in product delivery
organizational constraints team independence centralized ownership
summary: The post argues that operations (and ProductOps) should be approached as work on a sociotechnical system, not just a deterministic process or technology project. It contrasts experienced ops practices like safe-to-fail experiments, service design, and human factors with less experienced, process-centric approaches. It’s aimed at product and operations leaders trying to choose an effective ProductOps approach under real organizational constraints and different “motion” states (scaling vs fixing).

title: TBM 43A/52: More Stories About the Mess
questions: How do you operationalize product principles and frameworks across 10+ teams?
What does an operating model look like in practice (artifacts, rituals, documents, metrics)?
Why do teams fail when they apply frameworks only at the team level?
Where can product leaders find credible, reusable stories beyond a few famous case studies?
search_terms: Spotify model point in time snapshot
summary: The post argues that product organizations have an abundance of frameworks and principles but a shortage of real-world stories showing how to operationalize them at scale across many teams. It is aimed at product leaders and teams who are trying to adapt known models (e.g., empowered teams, team topologies, Spotify) into an actionable operating model, but lack detailed examples of artifacts, rituals, and processes.

title: TBM 29/53: Quicksand vs. Challenge
questions: Why do some product teams feel like they’re stuck in quicksand while others make steady progress?
Is a product transformation primarily a skills problem or an organizational constraints problem?
How do organizational inertia, outdated architecture, and proxy metrics prevent teams from operating independently?
What structural conditions enable junior designers and developers to ramp quickly and contribute?
search_terms: freedom and space for product teams
summary: The post contrasts “quicksand” team environments (firefighting, dependencies, inertia, and misaligned rituals/metrics) with “challenge” environments that still feel hard but produce momentum and impact. It argues that enterprise product transformation often fails because leaders treat it as a skills/practices gap rather than fixing structural constraints like architecture, customer access, strategy clarity, and measurement. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners trying to diagnose why teams struggle and what to change beyond training.

title: TBM 8/52: Avoid Mono-Process (But Embrace Shared Language)
questions: Should we use one process for all product work, or multiple processes?
When does standardizing processes for apples-to-apples comparisons backfire?
How do we create a work taxonomy that maps work types to appropriate processes?
How can we build shared language without forcing shared process?
search_terms: apples to apples comparison product work
summary: The post argues that product organizations should avoid a one-size-fits-all mono-process and instead support multiple ways of working based on the type of work. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who struggle with standardization, apples-to-apples comparisons, and inconsistent execution. The proposed approach is to build a company-specific work taxonomy and shared language, then define working agreements per work type.
